coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of Y type hexaferrite Sr2Ni2Fe12O22 supported carbon dots nanocomposite for enhanced water oxidation

coredata.dc:description: Water-splitting reactions, including the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), commonly require expensive noble metal-based electrocatalysts. This has inspired researchers to create innovative, economical electrocatalytic systems. The present study boons the fabrication of a novel multicomponent nanocomposite, integrating Sr2Ni2Fe12O22Y-type hexaferrites (YF), and carbon dots (CDs) and abbreviated as (YF@CDs). The YF@CDs exhibits a high porosity, multiple electroactive sites, rapid charge transfer, outstanding stability, and conductivity confirmed via various analytical techniques. The fabricated materials demonstrated exceptional performance in OER, exhibiting long-term durability of up to 40 h on a glassy carbon electrode. At a current density of 10 mA cm−2, this dual-function electrocatalyst demonstrates exceptional performance, with moderate overpotential values of 285 mV and 112 mV for OER and HER, and low Tafel slopes of 73 and 97 mV dec−1, respectively. Furthermore, it also shows decent turnover frequency (TOF) values (0.727 s−1, and 0.563 s−1 for OER and HER, respectively. It also has high mass activity value (574 mA mg−1 for OER and 222.4 mA mg−1 for HER). Furthermore, the resultant catalyst also shows high electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of 2750 cm2. Thus, this enhanced activity is due the good morphology, and large surface area of the nanocomposite material. This study presents a novel vision to investigate for the first time to indicate the boosted integrated performance between carbon-based materials along with YF. The unique features of individual component in this nanocomposite can be an interesting topic in the field of HER, OER, and many other electrochemical applications.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of Y type hexaferrite Sr2Ni2Fe12O22 supported carbon dots nanocomposite for enhanced water oxidation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-splitting reactions, including the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER), commonly require expensive noble metal-based electrocatalysts. This has inspired researchers to create innovative, economical electrocatalytic systems. The present study boons the fabrication of a novel multicomponent nanocomposite, integrating Sr2Ni2Fe12O22Y-type hexaferrites (YF), and carbon dots (CDs) and abbreviated as (YF@CDs). The YF@CDs exhibits a high porosity, multiple electroactive sites, rapid charge transfer, outstanding stability, and conductivity confirmed via various analytical techniques. The fabricated materials demonstrated exceptional performance in OER, exhibiting long-term durability of up to 40 h on a glassy carbon electrode. At a current density of 10 mA cm−2, this dual-function electrocatalyst demonstrates exceptional performance, with moderate overpotential values of 285 mV and 112 mV for OER and HER, and low Tafel slopes of 73 and 97 mV dec−1, respectively. Furthermore, it also shows decent turnover frequency (TOF) values (0.727 s−1, and 0.563 s−1 for OER and HER, respectively. It also has high mass activity value (574 mA mg−1 for OER and 222.4 mA mg−1 for HER). Furthermore, the resultant catalyst also shows high electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) of 2750 cm2. Thus, this enhanced activity is due the good morphology, and large surface area of the nanocomposite material. This study presents a novel vision to investigate for the first time to indicate the boosted integrated performance between carbon-based materials along with YF. The unique features of individual component in this nanocomposite can be an interesting topic in the field of HER, OER, and many other electrochemical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Green hydrogen production through water electrolysis stands as a highly promising path [1–3]. Water electrolysis comprises two half-reactions, specifically the cathodic HER, & anodic OER [4–7]. A theoretical voltage of 1.23 V is required to drive an overall water electrolysis device. Nevertheless, due to the sluggish reaction kinetics, mainly for the anodic OER, a high overpotential turn into a requirement to obtain a moderate current density [8–11]. Therefore, over the last few decades, significant advancements have been achieved in developing HER & OER electrocatalysts that are both cost-effective and high performing [12,13]. As of right now, Pt/C and IrO2/RuO2 systems serve as the foundation for the most sophisticated electrocatalysts for OER and HER [14,15]. These electrocatalysts are efficient, but they are not widely used due to their expensive cost and unstable long-term performance [16,17]. To address these issues, substantial research has been conducted to build dual-function electrocatalytic devices that use Earth-abundant metals while providing increased cost efficiency and sustainability.

High surface area and intrinsic catalytic activity are the two key parameters that determine the performance of an electrode in water splitting with low overpotential [18]. Due to their excellent efficiency in HER and OER, as well as their affordability and wide availability, transition metal-based composites have emerged as the most promising all-around water splitting catalysts in recent decades [19,20]. According to the research that have been published, transition metal ferrites that are plentiful on earth are considered among the many electrocatalysts that show the most promise for electrocatalytic activity for both OER and HER [21–23]. Nanoparticles of metal ferrites having general formula MFe2O4 (where M represents divalent metal ions including Ni, Mg, Mn, Cu, Co, Fe and others) have been widely studied because of their well-defined structure, superior magnetic property, high chemical stability and low cost [24,25]. Kubisztal et al. have discovered that the CoFe2O4 electrodes, which were made by a variety of deposition techniques incorporating nickel, demonstrated exceptional electrocatalytic characteristics for OER. When measured at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, the overpotential ranges from 287 to 295 mV [26]. Conversely, Silva et al. investigated 1D hollow MFe2O4 (M = Cu, Co, Ni) fibers produced by solution blow spinning for OER in alkaline solution using Tafel and EIS techniques. According to this work, hollow CuFe2O4 exhibits improved OER electrocatalytic activity and a larger ECSA [27].

On the other hand, Ferrites are classified into hard and flexible subclasses according to magnetic chemistry [28]. They are classified as garnet, spinel, orthogonal, and hexagonal ferrites according to their crystal structure, and are divided into six distinct kinds giving to their hexagonal structure and variable composition: M-type (BaFe12O19), W-type (BaMe2Fe16O27), U-type (Ba4Me2Fe36O60), X-type (Ba2Me2Fe28O46), Y-type (Ba2Me2 Fe12O22) and Z-type (Ba3Me2Fe24O41) where Me represent divalent cations [29]. Since spinel ferrite, a common soft magnetic ferritin, has low dc electrical resistivity, it cannot be used at the gigahertz frequency at which electronics devices operate due to an inherent limitation resulting from the occurrence of its natural ferromagnetic resonance at ultra-high frequency [30]. Recently M.Hussain et al. [31], developed SnFe2O4 by electrochemical Sm doping and obtained an overpotential of 179 mV and Tafel slope of 32 mAdec−1 for OER in alkaline medium. Their results were good but this is limited to OER only. Furthermore, Rajeshree et al. [32], prepared Ni–Co Doped M-type Strontium Hexaferrite and obtained overpotential of 465 mV and 103 mVdec−1 Tafel slope for OER & overpotential of 556 mV and 119 mVdec−1 for HER at 10 mA cm−2, which is much greater than traditional electrocatalysts RuO2 and Pt/C. Moreover, B Shalini et al. [33], designed CoFe2O4/gCN/NGQDs ternary composite for overall water splitting and attained overpotential of 287 and 445 mV at 10 mA cm−2. Similarly, Pitchai et al. [34], & Soham et al. [35], derived better results but still need improvement in lowering overpotential according to standard electrocatalysts.

Consequently, in applications over 1 GHz, hexaferrites have the potential to replace Y-type spinel ferrites. Recently, doping Y and M-type hexaferrites with bivalent or trivalent ions or their binary combinations has been attempted to alter their structural, electrical, and magnetic properties. Consequently, studies have been conducted on the substitution of multiple ions at both ferrous and divalent cation sites including: In Ref. [36], Al [37], Cu [38], Al-In Ref. [39], Mn [40], Al-Ga [41], Zn [42] etc. Therefore Y-type hexaferrites are very much less explored for electrolytic processes.

While these developments are significant, ferrites slow charge transfer kinetics and weak active sites still pose an issue. Rapid declines in rate performance are often caused by slow electron transport and a deficiency of active sites. Ferrites become much more efficient when carbon-based elements are added [43]. Among carbon materials, CDs have garnered a lot of attention in recent years due to their unique properties include a powerful and stable fluorescence, distinct electron transfer capabilities, an enormous surface area, a plentiful number of surface groups, cheapness and nontoxicity, etc [44,45].

Therefore, the current study reports the synthesis, characterization, and comprehensive catalytic application of a brand-new, unique nanocomposite that is designed to resemble Y-type hexaferrites (Sr2Ni2Fe12O22) combined with CDs. As expected, the electrocatalytic performance of composite heterostructures may be even better than that of single-phase materials, which is due to multiple active sites in addition to the synergistic effect of different phases [46]. It was found that the CDs obviously improved the electrochemical activity when combined with Y-type hexaferrites (YF) delivers the best OER and HER performance with the lowest overpotentials of 280 and 350 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, respectively. In this study, the unique physical and chemical characteristics of these materials contribute to their high electrocatalytic performance due to a change in morphology and crystal structure that improves stability.

The hydrothermal method was employed to produce CDs as reported already [47], which involved the combination of 32 mL of water and 40 mL of milk. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to vigorous agitation on a magnetic hotplate for 20 min. The milk and water mixture were then poured in the autoclave (100 mL capacity). After 2 h of growth at 150 °C in an electric oven, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. Then the precipitates were subjected to numerous cycles of centrifugation, purging, and rinsing with deionized water before being treated with ethanol. Subsequently, the product was retained for additional analysis following its drying at 80 °C.

The Y type hexaferrite Sr2Ni2Fe12O22 was synthesized using a simple micro-emulsion technique as reported already [48]. In order to generate the required quantities of solution, the precursor metal nitrates were dissolved in deionized water. The concentrations of nickel nitrate hexahydrate (0.017 M, 0.49 g), iron chloride (0.1 M, 1.62 g), and Strontium nitrate hexahydrate (0.0083 M, 0.26 g) were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water. CTAB was introduced as a surfactant at a metal: CTAB ratio of 1:1.5 % after the solutions were stirred on a magnetic heated plate. In this case, the CTAB functions as a cationic surfactant, stabilizing the microemulsion system and assuring homogeneous particle size and shape by forming reverse micelles that serve as nanoreactors for the regulated precipitation and growth of ferrite nanoparticles. After that, 2 M of KOH solution was added in a dropwise manner to ensure that the pH remained between 11 and 12. On the other hand, KOH acts as a precipitating agent, supplying the necessary hydroxide ions (OH−) for the formation of the ferrite phase. The mixture was agitated for an additional 2 h in order to attain homogeneity. The pH is reduced to 7 by centrifuging the precipitates and rinsing them with deionized water. These precipitates were then annealed in a furnace for 8 h at 900 °C after being desiccated in an oven at 100 °C.

The composite of the Y-type hexaferrite with the composition Sr2Ni2Fe12O22 and CDs was also fabricated by hydrothermal method. The same method was employed as for CDs with the addition of 0.2 g of already synthesized Y-type hexaferrite. The whole schematic diagram is shown in Scheme 1 .

The microstructure and surface morphology of the generated materials were investigated using a scanning electron microscope (Helios) throughout an energy range of 0–20 keV equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX). The X-ray diffractometer (XRD), Bruker-D8 equipment was used to record the sample diffraction patterns. To confirm the porous nature and the surface area of the fabricated materials Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) was employed.

All the electrochemical measurements, including controlled potential bulk electrolysis (CPE), EIS, LSV, and CV, were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (Autolab PGSTAT 204, Metrohm) with a traditional three-electrode electrochemical cell in a 1 M KOH solution within a Pyrex glass cell covered with Teflon. Before being used, cleaned the electrochemical cell by heating it to a boil in HNO3 and HCl (1:3) solution following rinsing it with ultrapure water and acetone. Finally, then dried it for 30 min at 80 °C. The glassy carbon as working electrode, Ag/AgCl (3.0 M KCl) as reference, and counter electrode (platinum wire) were used during three electrode system. The platinum wire in the electrochemical cell was also cleaned with a solution of nitric acid before use. The working electrode was prepared by making the slurry with the addition of 5 mg of the fabricated material in 100 μL of deionized water and also a Nafyon was used as a binding agent. All observed potentials against the RHE (reversible hydrogen electrode) were reported using following relation (1) [49,50].

At a scan rate of 5 mVs−1, 50 % iR-compensation was applied for recording the CV and LSV. The electrolyte solution was purged for approximately 30 min with argon gas before the measurement. The geometrical area of the working electrode was taken into account when calculating each current density.

The Tafel slope, which is utilized to evaluate the catalytic and kinetic efficiency indicated by the following equation (2), is computed by visualizing the linear component of the steady state polarization curve [51].

Where η is the overpotential, α represents the charge transfer coefficient, j is the current density, and F is the Faraday constant. Moreover, n is the total number of electrons that are used in the process. The slope formula is 2.303RT/αnF.

The parameters that are included in this equation are: A is the electrode's geometrical surface area; F is the Faraday constant; j is the current density in mA cm−2; and n is the total number of moles of available active catalyst, and 4, and 2 indicates the four and two electrons engaged in the multistep OER and HER processes, respectively.

Utilizing a CV curve at various scan rates (10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 mVs−1) was used to calculate the double layer capacitance; which is equivalent to the ECSA of the active electrodes. According to previously published data [32], the specific capacitance in the case of flat electrode is 0.04 mFcm −2, which is be divided by the double layer capacitance in order to calculate ECSA using equation (5) [53,54].

Across the electrode-electrolyte interphase, the solution resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) were investigated using the EIS approach. 0.1 Hz to 10 0,0 0 0 Hz is the frequency range covered by the method. These values correlate with the high frequency zone of the semicircle diameter of the Z′ against -Z″ Nyquist plot. The Metrohm Autolab electrochemical station's Rs and Rct values were found by using NOVA 2.1 software to match a simple Randles circuit. Every CV curve has a 50 % IR correction. Y-type hexaferrites/carbon dots are prepared by hydrothermal method as shown in Scheme 1 and the results of crystal structure and composition of the samples were determined by XRD. The XRD pattern of CDs, Y-type hexaferrites (YF) and Y- type hexaferrites@CDs (YF@CDs) were shown in Fig. 1 A to verify that synthesized materials have formed a Rhombohedral phase for the Y type hexaferrite. The diffractions peaks of YF at 2θ = 30.4°, 31.9°, 35.7°, 41.0° & 63.2° which are indexed at planes (110), (1013), (119), (0210) & (220) are closely matched with reference pattern of JCPDS No # 00-025-1193 having no residual peak confirming the single phase formation of synthesized composite [55]. Various parameters such as lattice constants and cell volume also calculated from XRD data reported in Table 1 . On the other hand, the pure CDs also shows a broad peak at 2θ = 20.06° (020) based on JCPDS card No. 00-049-1718, indicating the successful synthesis of the CDs. Furthermore, in YF@CDs peak, strong peaks correspond to the YF and a single peak at 2θ = 20.06° (020) is due to the presence of CDs which also indicate the successful formation of the composite. The crystalline sizes of CDs, YF and YF@CDs calculated from Sherer equation [56] as in equation (9) found to be 0.70 nm, 24.6 nm and 19.6 nm respectively. The small size of nanocomposite leads to the large surface area which is beneficial for catalytic application.
(9)
Crystalline

( D )

k λ

COS

Where D is the crystallite size, β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM), θ is the Bragg's angle, and λ is the X-ray wavelength. Scherer's constant, K, characterizes the shape of the crystal [57].

The surface morphology and particle sized of all the synthesized materials are examined with the aid of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) as shown in Fig. 1 at different resolutions. Fig. 1B-C shows the SEM morphology of CDs with low and high magnification levels with clear cluster shape morphology. The SEM morphology of YF in Fig. 1 (D and E) presents spherical and irregular shaped large cluster of particles. Results indicated the planer geometries and uniform particles distribution of YF particles and nano-clustered morphology occur due to Van-der wall forces and magnetic interaction between the particles [58]. In case of YF @CDs composite Fig. 1(F–H) reveals smaller, more homogenous particles with porous structure of CDs and heterojunction formed between CDs and YF particles. This heterojunction is so complex that it’s difficult to recognize due to porous nature of CDs. The CDs act as capping agent which hinders agglomeration and regulates the growth of the ferrite particles At the same time it is observed that numerous active sites are formed which are responsible for remarkable electrocatalytic activity of composite [59].

The elemental mapping images of composite revealed the uniform distribution of (C), (Fe), (Ni), (O) and (Sr) elements as shown in Fig. 2 A-F, mapping overly image in present images also confirmed the homogeneity of composite making it a suitable choice for various catalytic applications. The EDX elemental map of YF@CDs provided in Fig. 2G indicates that C, Fe, Sr, O and Ni are evenly distributed throughout the material. Furthermore, it is quite obvious that the mole percentages calculated from the EDX spectrum presented in Fig. 2G (inset) are quite close to the theoretical values.

Fig. 3 depicts the surface analysis of CDs and YF@CDs with BET and pore size distributions. BET isotherms Fig. 3(A & B) show a considerable increase in surface area from 5.146 m2/g for CDs to 17.191 m2/g for YF@CDs, indicating higher porosity. The pore size distribution Fig. 3(C & D) demonstrates that YF@CDs have a broader and more defined mesoporous structure than CDs. This shows that adding CDs improves surface properties, making YF@CDs better suited for catalytic or electrochemical applications.

The electrocatalytic performance for OER was tested for all the CDs, YF, YF@CDs samples using the LSV measurement in 1 M KOH. Polarization curves of CDs, YF & YF@CDs compared with traditional reference electrocatalyst IrO2, were shown in Fig. 4 A. It was found that coupling of CDs with YF give best electrochemical performance. According to the results represented in Fig. 4, the following order describes the OER activity: YF@CDs has higher activity than YF, and CDs. When the thermodynamic potential was measured at 10 mA cm−2 current density, the YF@CDs showed the lowest onset potential of 1.41 V versus RHE. It was found that coupling of YF@CDs gives best electrochemical performance. The CDs most likely contribute to enhanced OER performance and an increased electron transfer rate by offering a conductive channel and a larger defect structure for both the adsorption and desorption of transitory species [60]. Further, comparison of overpotentials of all samples are shown in Fig. 4 (B). The results acquired from the experiment reveal that at the current density of 10 mA cm−2, the YF@CDs sample demonstrates the least value of overpotential of 285 mV vs IrO2 (230 mV). The overpotentials of other samples under similar conditions are 495 mV (YF), 550 mV (CDs) & 590 mV for Bare GC displayed in (Fig. 4B). There are several factors that can be attributed to the higher performance of the YF@CDs electrocatalyst when compared to other samples:(a) the presence of multiple electroactive sites as represented by Fe, and Ni, Sr metallic centers (b) enhanced conductivity as a result of CDs incorporation and (c) structural porosity and stability [61].

The kinetic aspects of the behavior of electrocatalysts were also investigated. Plotting the resulting Tafel curves for this purpose was done using the following equation, which was obtained from the LSV curves: η = βlog (j/j0). Here, β stands for the Tafel slope, j represents the current density, and the subscript "j0" denotes the current density at zero overpotential [62]. High exchange current density and low Tafel slope are the two characteristics that this equation suggests the formation of successful electrocatalyst. The Tafel slopes that were determined as a result are 173 mV dec−1 (bare GC), 145 mV dec−1 (CDs), 136 mV dec−1 (YF), 73 mV dec−1 (YF@CDs) and 65 mV dec−1 (IrO2). This analysis of these values of Tafel slope further confirm that, the Tafel slope value of YF@CDs is significantly lower relative to the related designed catalysts, reflecting the improved kinetics of the reaction. To investigate the kinetic characteristics of electrocatalysis in OER, EIS measurements were conducted. The samples' Nyquist plots are shown in (Fig. 4D). The picture shows that, in comparison to other samples, YF (420 Ω) & CDs (599 Ω), the electrode from YF@CDs has the lowest charge-transfer resistance (374 Ω). Therefore, suggesting a superior electrocatalytic performance of the electrode as compared to the others. Moreover, Nyquist plots of YF@CDs reveal the smaller semicircle, and reduced Rct, clearly suggesting that the electrocatalyst performs extremely well. Such an efficient electrocatalytic activity can be explained by the presence of CDs and synergistic effect on their interaction [63,64]. The resistance of the solution is denoted by Rs in the analogous Randle circuit seen as the inset in Fig. 4D for the nanocomposite.

Catalytic activities for fabricated YF and their composite to split H2O into H2 and O2 under ambient condition per unit time were also analyzed using a fabulous technique called TOF. The value for TOF is larger, it implies that the fabricated materials hold tremendous electrochemical activity it also implies that the efficiency is high. TOF values of CDs, YF, YF@CDs are (0.0008 s−1), (0.07006 s−1) and (0.7271 s−1) displayed in Fig. 4E. From these TOF values, it could be inferred that YF@CDs reached the highest value in comparison with other fabricated materials. That was the reason for the high TOF value, which resulted into excellent OER capacity hence endowing it with excellent activity. These changes facilitate the availability and distribution of active sites and is crucial for the effectiveness of OER [65].

The superior catalytic activity among as prepared catalysts can be found by comparing their mass activities, which delivers information about the specific amount of mass required to produce maximum current density. Like TOF, the mass activity of the electrode was also calculated at an overpotential of 350 mV, and a bar chart of comparison can be seen in Fig. 4F. Among all the catalysts, the YF@CDs had the highest mass activity of 574 mA mg−1, which indicates the availability of most active sites on this catalyst. However, the YF and CDs showed a smaller mass activities of 56 and 52 mA mg−1, respectively, and lower OER performance in consequence.

The long-time chrono-potentiometric test (j vs t) of the optimized YF@CDs electrode at specific current density is depicted in Fig. 4G. As has been expected the YF@CDs exhibits excellent operational stability in terms of their activity towards 40 h of continuous running without any loss in current during this duration at benchmark current density of 10 mA cm−2. It was also mentioned that electrocatalysts have an advantage by revealing that new active sites are still generated as the OER reaction advances even further and the catalyst maintains a greater longevity of activity.

For OER in alkaline media, active species such as the hydroxide ions (OH−) are adsorbed to the catalyst surface shown in Fig. 4H. The YF@CDs surface works as a catalyst to enable the reaction. It starts with the adsorption of hydroxide ions at the surface of the catalyst. These ions first passed through oxidation where they lose electrons (e−) to produce oxygen intermediates like OH∗ and O∗ which are temporarily adsorbed on the catalyst surface [66,67]. During the reaction these intermediates join together to from molecular oxygen (O2). They are then released from the surface of the catalyst, thus the OER process has been completed [68]. The catalyst structure also enables the diffusion of reactants (OH−) into the active site and the stabilization of reaction intermediates together with efficient pathways for electron transfer so that the reaction occurs freely. The presence of CDs increases the catalyst conductivity and activity with the help of the Sr/Ni that maintain the intermediates and accelerate the electron transfer to oxidize the OH−.

In addition to OER, electrocatalytic activity of HER has also examined under same conditions like OER. LSV curves of YF@CDs, YF and CDs and their comparison with Pt/C electrocatalyst are shown in Fig. 5 A. Plotting of the resulting Tafel curves (Fig. 5B) produced from the LSV diagrams was done using the same equation as OER: η = β log(j/j0). Kinetic features of the behavior of electrocatalysts were also examined [69,70]. When employing these catalysts for the HER reaction, the Volmer–Heyrovsky mechanism is also indicated by the Tafel slopes for Pt/C, YF@CDs, YF and CDs of 88, 97, 179, and 237 mV dec−1, respectively. Tafel slope of YF@CDs is close to traditional electrocatalyst. Additionally, the electrochemical H2 gas desorption could be a phase that determines rate of the reaction. With regard to CDs, the Tafel slope of 237 mVdec−1 suggested that the Volmer step predominates and restricts the pace of reaction, resulting in subpar HER characteristics in this substance. However, the Tafel slope has an impact on the HER mechanism. A hydrogen molecule is created from a proton during the Volmer, Heyrovsky, and Tafel phases [71,72], which is produced by the combination of Equations 10–12
(10) Volmer Step: M + H+ + e− → M−Hads
(11) Heyrovsky Step: H+ + M−Hads + e− → M + H2
(12) Tafel Step: M−Hads + M−Hads → M + H2

Moreover, from LSV results, we have analyzed overpotential of Pt/C, YF@CDs, YF & CDs at10 mA cm−2. Consequently, 0.12 V vs RHE (onset potential) required to start the reaction and produce hydrogen bubbles and it is the least possible voltage of YF@CDs as compared Pt/C required only 0.092 V vs RHE which is closer to YF@CDs, and onset potential of YF (0.513V) and CDs (0.64V) which is greater than our composite material. In the context of OER analysis, the ECSA measurement gives information about the kind and the degree of the catalyst surface area accessible for facilitating the OER processes at the electrode surface. Fig. 5D represents ECSA values of all samples.

Further TOF has been calculated for HER as shown in Fig. 5E. The TOF of an electrocatalyst is a kinetic parameter that represents catalytic ability of the electrocatalyst to initiate the electrochemical process in order to achieve the activity of the developed materials. The YF@CDs exhibited greater TOF (0.563 s−1) and it outperforms than the other YF (0.198 s−1) and CDs (0.0007 s−1) electrocatalysts. High TOF value will boost up the activity of fabricated material and this is due to synergistic interaction between YF and CDs and it ultimately possess remarkable catalytic activity and have high number of active sites. Also, YF@CDs exhibited higher mass activity (222.4 mA mg−1) as compared to other samples, YF (79.2 mA mg−1) & CDs (24.8 mA mg−1), respectively. Their comparison of bar chart shown in Fig. 5F.

Another critical characteristic for electrocatalytic activity of the material is its stability [64]. As demonstrated in Fig. 5G, chrono-potentiometric measurement at a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2 was designed to test the HER stability of YF@CDs, just like OER. After 30 h of continuous action, the YF@CDs showed good stability, retaining around 94 % of their activity. In steady state conditions, this is one of the better documented OER activities on glassy carbon electrodes (GCE). The obstruction of the GCE's 0.07 cm2 exposed surface area by the produced O2 bubbles, however, may be the cause of the activity decline after 30 h [73]. Hence supporting the fact that YF@CDs can be effectively employed as good, efficient electrocatalyst for OER in comparison to other similar electrocatalysts with much better durability for long term use.

Fig. 5H showing the reaction mechanism of HER and it starts with the adsorption of water molecules on the catalyst surface (Volmer step) which consist of hexaferrite materials with CDs. Upon reaching the surface, water breaks up into its constituents, hydroxide ions and protons and OH− ions are free to move in the solution and the protons H+ in turn remain adsorbed on catalyst surface as hydrogen intermediates Hads [74]. These protons then gain electrons (e−) from the catalyst, leading to the formation of hydrogen atoms (Hads)(Heyrovsky step) [75]. As more hydrogen atoms accumulate on the surface, they combine to form molecular hydrogen (H2). The hydrogen molecules are then released from the catalyst surface, and thus HER process has been completed (Tafel step) [76].

Another significant factor when it comes to electrocatalytic activity involves a high ECSA calculated via CV curves as shown in Fig. 6 (a–c). Thus, this parameter has to be investigated. Accordingly, the calculation of ECSA confirmed that there is a direct correlation between Cdl and ECSA [77]. In order to examine this important feature, we repeated the CV test in the non-Faradic potential range at different scan rates. This work examined the electrocatalysts' ECSA in order to verify that YF@CDs outperform OER & HER. Out of all the catalysts (Fig. 6F), the YF@CDs electrocatalyst had the highest Cdl value (110 μF). We can obtain a large amount of current density for the electrocatalysts as a function of scan rate by plotting CV curves. Consequently, the electrocatalyst that exhibits the best electrocatalytic behavior is the one with the highest Cdl since they will have the largest ECSA. All the investigated catalysts in the current study are compared in terms of the previous results presented in Table 2 which is found that YF@CDs is the most suitable catalyst with respect to previous designed catalysts.

As a result, we propose that the outstanding performance of YF@CDs as a novel bifunctional electrocatalyst for OER/HER can be explained as follows: (1) The intrinsic characteristics of CDs and the YF composite enhance ion transport by generating a unique structure with adequate electrical conductivity and stability [88]. (3) So, the porous nature of the nanocomposite has created a unique optimal spatial arrangement and enhance number of active sites as confirmed via BET surface area and pore size, that either improves reactant accessibility for water molecule adsorption and desorption and CDs provide protective layer for the prevention of YF degradation.

Overall, we reported a cost effective and simple strategy for Y-Type hexaferrites with CDs (YF@CDs) as a highly efficient and durable bifunctional electrocatalyst for OER and HER processes. The Y-type hexaferrites and incorporation of highly conductive CDs enhances the carrier transport. Alternatively, the performance of this electrocatalyst toward OER and HER is enhanced due to the presence of active metal centers (Sr, Fe, Ni) as electroactive sites. A remarkable catalytic behavior of YF@CDs/GC in a 1 M KOH electrolyte solution can be explained by its increased surface area, improved electrical conduction and, consequently, accelerated charge transfer and accessibility of electroactive sites. The nanocomposite possessed lesser overpotentials as well as Tafel slopes for OER (285 mV and 73 mV dec−1) and HER (112 mV and 97 mV dec−1) at 10 mA cm−2, than other bifunctional catalysts. The TOF values for an OER and HER are 0.727 s−1 and 0.563 s−, respectively. Additionally, the catalytic activity is enhanced by the high ECSA of 2750 cm2. Furthermore, this electrocatalyst demonstrated good stability up to 40 h during the OER process. The current multi-interface engineering method opens up new avenues for developing future dual-function electrocatalysts based on tri- or multimetallic composites with high conductivity, stability, and advanced water-splitting capabilities. However, future study should concentrate on scaling up the production of the YF@CDs nanocomposite to determine its industrial relevance, as well as investigating its integration into a comprehensive water-splitting electrolyzer to assess real-world performance and long-term operating stability.
